stories.title: West Palm Beach Eyeing Waterfront Property Moratorium
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach gave tentative approval to a measure that would pause redevelopment in a waterfront area of the city’s downtown. It will mostly affect the seven apartment and condo projects on S. Flagler Drive and Washington Road. The S. Florida Business Journal reports the measure would halt any new projects in the area that require rezoning. Developments filed earlier wouldn’t be affected. The measure comes amid growing community pushback against the scale and speed of redevelopment in West Palm Beach’s urban core in recent years. The six-month pause would give the city time to hire an outside consultant to recommend potential zoning changes to the area, which is home to a number of vintage multifamily and condominium buildings (shown) that could be ripe for luxury redevelopment. Before the moratorium can go into effect, the ordinance would require a second reading by city commissioners. That vote is slated for July 20. The post West Palm Beach Eyeing Waterfront Property Moratorium appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: West Palm Beach approves six-month moratorium on waterfront rezoning applications south of Monroe Drive
Pause affects seven apartment/condo projects on S. Flagler Drive; filed projects exempt
City hiring consultant for zoning analysis; second vote scheduled July 20
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners gave tentative approval to a six-month moratorium on new waterfront development applications in downtown, affecting seven planned apartment and condo projects along the S. Flagler Drive corridor. The pause—set to expire January 20, 2027—allows the city to hire a zoning consultant and study redevelopment patterns. Projects already filed are grandfathered; a final vote is scheduled for July 20.
stories.impact: This moratorium creates a window of uncertainty and potential deal acceleration in West Palm Beach before January 2027, favoring developers with entitled sites. More importantly, it signals municipal pushback against luxury-focused redevelopment and signals demand for affordability/preservation—a regulatory headwind for value-add acquisition play in the waterfront submarket and a sign that future entitlements may face political friction.

stories.title: Peerless Building 259-Unit NCSU Student Housing Development
stories.excerpt: Peerless Development is pursuing the construction of a 713-bed student housing project on Crest Road directly behind North Carolina State University’s South Campus. The Triangle Business Journal reports that the site plans show a 7-story mixed-use building — the height would range from 5 to 7 stories due to grade changes on the site. There will be 259 units in total, with 50 one-bedroom units, 80 two-bedroom units, 23 three-bedroom units, 96 four-bedroom units, and 10 five-bedroom units. The project will be two levels of underground parking with 294 parking spaces. The only amenity detailed in the plans is a swimming pool. Peerless expects to add a retail component to the project. The site is 2.57 acres at 1523 Crest Road, the current location of an apartment complex (shown), which must be razed before construction begins. Peerless Development specializes in multifamily, student housing and industrial development and acquisition. The post Peerless Building 259-Unit NCSU Student Housing Development appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Peerless Development pursuing 259-unit, 713-bed student housing project at NCSU South Campus
Five- to seven-story mixed-use building with 294 parking spaces; site requires razed existing apartment complex
Unit mix: 96 four-bed, 80 two-bed, 50 one-bed, 23 three-bed, 10 five-bed units
stories.summary: Peerless Development is planning a 259-unit, 713-bed student housing project on 2.57 acres directly behind North Carolina State University's South Campus in Raleigh. The mixed-use, five- to seven-story building will replace an existing apartment complex and include 294 parking spaces, underground parking, a pool, and retail. The unit mix heavily favors four-bedroom units (96 of 259), reflecting student household demand.
stories.impact: Student housing at NCSU—a major research institution with 34,000+ students—signals strong, steady-state demand for beds rather than traditional apartments. This project demonstrates continued developer confidence in university-adjacent infill in Raleigh, a Sunbelt market with persistent enrollment and job growth. The high concentration of four-beds (37% of units) reflects a specific student demand pattern that differs from traditional multifamily, lowering lease-up risk but constraining adult/family repositioning optionality.

stories.title: Center for Justice Innovation Expands to 34K SF at GFP’s 520 Eighth Avenue
stories.excerpt: The Center for Justice Innovation, a nonprofit organization focused on community safety and racial justice, is taking more space at its Midtown offices. The nonprofit has signed a deal to expand to 34,300 square feet at GFP Real Estate’s 520 Eighth Avenue and extend its current lease at the property, the landlord announced Thursday. The 20-year lease spans a portion of the 18th floor and the entire 19th floor of the 26-story Midtown office building. The Center for Justice Innovation has been a tenant at the property since 1999 and has expanded within the building over the years, most recently occupying 33,000 square feet on parts of the 10th, 18th and 23rd floors. This new lease gives the company an extra 1,300 square feet and consolidates the organization’s footprint in the building over two floors. “Center for Justice Innovation has been part of the 520 Eighth Avenue community for more than 25 years, and we’re proud to continue supporting the organization through this next phase of growth,” GFP’s Matthew Mandell, who represented the landlord in-house, said in a statement. “This transaction further reinforces 520 Eighth Avenue’s position as one of Manhattan’s premier hubs for mission driven organizations.” The asking rent was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office properties in the Penn District was $70.09 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to research from Newmark. Open Impact Real Estate’s Stephen Powers and Jake Cinti represented the tenant in the deal. “As the role of the workplace continues to evolve, nonprofits are increasingly approaching real estate as a strategic tool to deepen impact and support long-term sustainability,” Cinti said in a statement. “By consolidating the Center for Justice Innovation’s operations into a modernized and highly efficient headquarters, this transaction not only positions the organization for continued growth and long-term savings, but creates a workplace environment that reflects
bullets: Center for Justice Innovation expands to 34.3K SF across two floors at 520 Eighth Avenue, Manhattan
20-year lease renewal consolidates tenant from five floors into contiguous 18th-19th floor space
Nonprofit gains 1.3K SF while landlord improves asset utilization and rental stability
stories.summary: The Center for Justice Innovation, a nonprofit tenant since 1999, expanded its lease at GFP Real Estate's 520 Eighth Avenue in Midtown Manhattan to 34,300 square feet on the 18th and 19th floors, gaining 1,300 square feet. The 20-year renewal consolidates the tenant's fragmented footprint from five floors into two contiguous floors, improving operational efficiency and landlord asset management.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is an office transaction in a historically office-heavy Manhattan asset; it reflects stable nonprofit demand and landlord strategy to consolidate underutilized tenancies, but signals no broader Manhattan market trends affecting residential supply, demand, or capital flows.

stories.title: Investor Duo Spending $20M on Charlotte Office Tower Revamp
stories.excerpt: Trinity Capital Advisors and Town Lane kicked off the makeover of the 15-story, 388,657-square-foot office tower at 440 S. Church St. The Charlotte Business Journal reports the $20 million project will focus on the 17-year-old building’s two lobbies and include upgrades to existing tenant amenities, common areas and conference rooms at the office tower on 440 South Church. The building is less than 50% leased. The two investors bought the building from Epic Investment Partners in October 2025 for about $75.8 million. Trinity Capital originally developed the tower, which delivered in 2009. The renovations will encompass 21,000 square feet of the building’s interior as well as significant street-level improvements, Collier said. Work is expected to be completed in the second quarter of 2027. Additionally, the firms are building out about 24,000 square feet of speculative suites on floors nine and 10. Redline Design Group is the project architect and designer, and Choate Construction is the general contractor. The post Investor Duo Spending $20M on Charlotte Office Tower Revamp appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Trinity Capital Advisors and Town Lane acquire Charlotte office tower for $75.8M; investing $20M renovation
388.7K SF Class B building at 440 S. Church St. less than 50% leased; 21K SF interior + street-level remodel
Speculative build-out of 24K SF suites on floors 9-10; completion targeted Q2 2027
stories.summary: Trinity Capital Advisors and Town Lane purchased a 388,657-square-foot, 15-story office tower in Charlotte for $75.8 million in October 2025 and are investing $20 million in a comprehensive repositioning. The building—currently less than 50% leased—will receive lobby, amenity, and street-level upgrades plus speculative suites on floors 9-10, with work slated for completion in Q2 2027.
stories.impact: Deep distress in Charlotte office fundamentals (sub-50% occupancy, steep discount to 2009 delivery cost) reflects national office crisis but has minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, the $20M repositioning bet signals landlord confidence in Charlotte's core recovery and suggests employment stabilization in the market—a positive secondary indicator for residential demand growth but not an immediate supply or capital driver for apartment development.

stories.title: U.S. Industrial Market Tightens as New Deliveries Diminish
stories.excerpt: U.S. industrial real estate continued to strengthen in 2026’s second quarter as demand and supply were in balance, pushing the national vacancy rate below 7% while leasing activity reached its highest level since mid-2022, Cushman & Wakefield said in its latest report. Year-to-date, occupiers have absorbed 113.6 million square feet of industrial space, the strongest first-half performance since 2023. At the same time, industrial vacancy declined to 6.9% as new deliveries remained relatively modest. Developers completed 62 million square feet of new space in Q2, bringing first-half deliveries to 119 million square feet, nearly 20% below the year-ago period. “After several quarters of market recalibration, the U.S. industrial sector is entering a new phase characterized by healthier fundamentals and more balanced growth,” said Jason Tolliver, president, Logistics & Industrial Americas, at Cushman & Wakefield. “Occupier demand continues to build while development has become much more disciplined than it was during the peak construction cycle. That combination is beginning to tighten market conditions once again, particularly for newer, high-quality logistics facilities.” The post U.S. Industrial Market Tightens as New Deliveries Diminish appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: U.S. industrial vacancy fell to 6.9% in Q2 2026; leasing absorbed 113.6M SF year-to-date
New deliveries slowed to 62M SF in Q2, bringing first-half completions to 119M SF (down 20% year-over-year)
Market tightening pushes developers to discipline; occupier demand building across sectors
stories.summary: U.S. industrial real estate tightened significantly in Q2 2026 as supply and demand balanced. National vacancy fell below 7% to 6.9%, while year-to-date leasing absorption reached 113.6 million square feet—the strongest first-half since 2023. Crucially, new deliveries declined 20% year-over-year to 119 million square feet in the first half, signaling developers have cut back construction after the 2021–2024 boom.
stories.impact: Industrial tightening signals sustained logistics demand (likely e-commerce, nearshoring, reshoring) across major metros, which indirectly supports population and employment growth in gateway and secondary markets—a positive underlying signal for multifamily demand. However, the slowdown in industrial starts may dampen construction-labor costs and availability, temporarily improving multifamily project economics. Monitor industrial market for capacity constraints and wage pressure that could spill into apartment construction labor costs in tight industrial metros (Dallas, Atlanta, Phoenix).

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami secures $193M permit for 257K SF civic center at Freedom Park; topping-off July 10
Eight-story building combines city administrative functions; $20M pedestrian bridge to Metrorail under construction
130-acre Freedom Park development anchored by Inter Miami CF stadium; completion expected spring 2028
stories.summary: Miami's new civic center, part of the $1 billion Freedom Park mixed-use development near Miami International Airport, received a $193 million construction permit and topped off in July. The 257,000-square-foot, eight-story building will consolidate city government functions and connect via a $20 million pedestrian bridge to a nearby Metrorail station. The broader 130-acre development is anchored by Inter Miami CF's stadium and is slated for completion by spring 2028.
stories.impact: Freedom Park is a major transit-oriented, mixed-use anchor development signaling significant municipal and private capital flowing to the MIA airport submarket. The Metrorail connection and stadium anchor will drive population concentration and demand for both market-rate and workforce housing near the station. Developers should track Freedom Park's retail, office, and hotel deliveries for residential absorption patterns and renter demand near transit; this is a flagship placemaking project that may outpace traditional multifamily absorption curves and attract lifestyle-focused renters.

stories.title: CMBS Special Servicing Rate Reverses May’s Monthly Decline
stories.excerpt: The Trepp CMBS special servicing rate increased by 34 basis points in June to 11.20%, reversing May’s decline. Special servicing rates increased in four of the six major property types in June, reported Trepp. Lodging posted the largest increase, rising 44 bps to 8.89%, and office climbed 36 bps to 17.11%, also led by several large transfers. Mixed-use rose 28 bps to 11.90%, and industrial edged up nine bps to 1.37% Multifamily posted the biggest improvement, falling 27 bps to 8.23%, aided by the large Yorkshire & Lexington Towers loan return. Retail edged down five bps to 12.95%. In June, new transfers totaled roughly $3.08 billion across 42 loans. The largest loan to transfer in June was the $975-million IMC Portfolio loan, which transferred for imminent balloon/maturity default. It’s backed by a 16-property, 9.6-million-square-foot showroom portfolio in Las Vegas and High Point, NC, with properties constructed between 1907 and 2017. The post CMBS Special Servicing Rate Reverses May’s Monthly Decline appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CMBS special servicing rate rose 34 bps in June to 11.20%; office jumped 36 bps to 17.11%
Multifamily improved 27 bps to 8.23%, aided by Yorkshire & Lexington Towers loan return to performing
New transfers totaled $3.08B across 42 loans; lodging rose 44 bps to 8.89%
stories.summary: The CMBS special servicing rate increased 34 basis points in June to 11.20%, reversing May's decline. Multifamily showed the strongest improvement, falling 27 basis points to 8.23% following a large loan return to performing status. Office posted the largest increase at 36 basis points to 17.11%, reflecting continued distress; new transfers totaled $3.08 billion across 42 loans.
stories.impact: Multifamily special servicing improvement signals stabilization of CMBS multifamily collateral, likely reflecting 2023–2024 rent growth and rate resilience. However, office and lodging distress (17.11% and 8.89% respectively) indicate capital stress and loan transfers accelerating in competing property types. For multifamily developers, this suggests capital for multifamily remains available (lower special servicing rate = lower default risk = better debt terms), while office-to-residential conversion or development may face tighter financing conditions if mixed-use sponsors are capital-constrained.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related California delivers 700 Broadway luxury project in Santa Monica with 70K SF amenity space
Three-building, mixed-cladding design surrounds private 0.75-acre courtyard; rooftop pools, sauna, hot tubs, BBQ
Interior amenities include Speakeasy-style bar, handmade materials; target resident seeks lifestyle integration
stories.summary: Related California completed 700 Broadway, a luxury residential project in Santa Monica anchored by 70,000 square feet of curated amenity space. The three-building development—two in brick, one in glass/metal—surrounds a 0.75-acre private courtyard and rooftop recreation areas (dual pools, hot tubs, sauna, BBQ). Interior amenities emphasize lifestyle design, including The Reserve speakeasy bar and handcrafted finishes targeting affluent, experience-driven residents.
stories.impact: 700 Broadway exemplifies the "amenity arms race" in West LA luxury markets, where lifestyle programming and outdoor resort-style space command pricing power. The project's 70K SF amenity footprint (likely 15–20% of total GBA) signals that Santa Monica luxury renters willingly trade unit square footage for curated common space and experiential design. Developers targeting affluent coastal/gateway markets should note this shift: amenity-dense, lower unit-count designs may achieve higher rents-per-SF despite smaller individual units, but require high-level design and programming expertise.

stories.title: The New Stakeholder in Data Center Development
stories.excerpt: A new stakeholder has entered the data center development process. It does not appear on a cap table. It does not sign a lease. But it is now shaping outcomes as much as capital, land and power. The anti-data center movement is no longer a fringe reaction. It is organized, informed and increasingly effective. Consider Missouri, where organized opposition groups are relatively few, but claim more combined members than any other state in the country — more than triple the next closest. Small in number, enormous in reach. That is not a grassroots reaction. That is an organized constituency operating with real discipline. Suhail Y Tayeb. PHOTO: Courtesy NYU Or consider Utah, where Kevin O’Leary’s Stratos data center project was originally planned to span an area roughly twice the size of Manhattan. After weeks of resident protests and a formal letter from the state Senate president, the company cut its footprint by three quarters and its own leadership admitted the rollout had been mishandled from the start. That is not a story about one developer’s mistake. It is a story about what a coordinated stakeholder can now do to a fully financed project, regardless of its economics. Across the country, projects are being delayed, restructured or canceled not because demand is weak, but because opposition is strong. Local groups are coordinating across states, sharing playbooks, and learning how to influence zoning boards, utility commissions and elections. More than 100 local communities have already enacted their own moratoriums, and they did so independently of one another, without any centralized campaign coordinating the effort.What began as isolated project-level opposition has evolved into a broader network of communities comparing experiences, strategies and outcomes. This is not a temporary phase. It is a structural shift in how infrastructure gets approved. For years, data center development followed a familiar pattern. Secure land, line up power, mo
bullets: Organized anti-data center opposition movements shaping development outcomes across U.S.; Missouri groups claim record combined membership
Kevin O'Leary's Stratos project in Utah cut footprint by 75% after state Senate opposition and resident protests
Anti-data center constituency now a material stakeholder in site selection, entitlements, and feasibility
stories.summary: Organized anti-data center opposition movements are increasingly affecting development feasibility and project scope across the U.S. Missouri anti-data center groups claim combined membership exceeding any other state—more than triple the next closest. In Utah, O'Leary's Stratos data center project was forced to cut its planned footprint by three-quarters following coordinated resident and state Senate opposition, signaling a new material constraint on industrial development.
stories.impact: This trend indirectly affects multifamily by reshaping land availability and municipal bandwidth: as data centers face local opposition and entitlement delays, competing industrial uses (warehouses, logistics) may intensify competition for sites, and jurisdictions may pivot industrial/employment zones toward residential or mixed-use. However, the anti-data center movement also reflects underlying community and environmental concerns (power, water, noise) that may constrain any large-footprint industrial/residential development. Developers should monitor data center opposition in target markets as an indicator of municipal/resident appetite for high-intensity development and regulatory risk.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach commissioners approve second reading vote on waterfront moratorium, July 20
Moratorium applies to S. Flagler Drive corridor; 12+ luxury condo projects totaling 2K+ units in pipeline
Related Group, Terra, BH Group driving Miami-based condo development; Steve Ross leading downtown buildout
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved the waterfront moratorium on first reading and scheduled a final vote for July 20. The six-month pause affects high-rise developments in downtown's Flagler Drive corridor. More than a dozen luxury condo projects totaling 2,000+ units are in the works, led by Miami-based developers including Related Group, Terra, and BH Group, with Steve Ross spearheading the buildout.
stories.impact: The large pipeline of luxury condo projects (12+ projects, 2K+ units) reflects speculative overheating in West Palm Beach's downtown waterfront, driven by Miami-based capital chasing limited infill sites. The moratorium will create a hard deadline (January 2027) for entitlements and a potential deal acceleration before the pause takes effect. For apartment developers, this signals that condo-for-sale projects are crowding out rental supply in this submarket; any multifamily projects should target workforce/attainable segments or secondary locations outside the Flagler corridor to avoid entitlement delays and political friction.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposes 110-unit affordable housing at former Wells Fargo site, 295 South Mathilda Avenue, Sunnyvale
All units income-restricted except manager's unit; 25% earmarked for previously unhoused; area median income $143.9K
Proposal reflects Sunnyvale emergence as multifamily destination as AI boom drives tech worker demand
stories.summary: MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing development in Sunnyvale at a former Wells Fargo bank site. All units except the manager's unit will be income-restricted, with 25% designated for previously unhoused individuals. The project reflects Sunnyvale's emergence as a multifamily destination as AI-driven employment growth and housing demand spill out from San Francisco into Silicon Valley.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale is experiencing secondary tech-worker migration and demand spillover from San Francisco, driven by AI/Big Tech expansion and constrained Bay Area housing. The presence of affordable housing development alongside market-rate projects (Beam Reach's 265-unit office-to-residential conversion nearby) signals strong, broad-based residential demand across income segments. Developers should view Sunnyvale as an increasingly competitive infill market where entitlements are moving (mixed-use, conversion-friendly zoning) and rents are climbing relative to San Francisco. The AI boom tailwind suggests sustained job growth; monitor employment data and tech HQ announcements in Sunnyvale/Santa Clara for early-stage demand signals.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M for fraud scheme over fake 'District' luxury development
Pair defrauded Georgetown business owner of $21M contract for infrastructure on 400-acre project never intended to build
Scheme involved dual pitches—small plan to seller, large plan with inflated Army appeal to European bankers
stories.summary: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages for defrauding Georgetown business owner Marshall Hussain over a nonexistent luxury development called "The District." The pair obtained a $21 million infrastructure contract for a 400-acre waterfront development, made only symbolic payments, and used dual marketing pitches to solicit a $5 billion bond from European banks. This follows a 2023 SEC action against the pair for an alleged Ponzi scheme.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. This is a fraud/criminal case with no operational real estate development significance. However, it is a cautionary signal about speculative development promotion in Texas and the necessity of due diligence on founders/sponsors claiming large mixed-use projects, particularly waterfront/resort developments marketed to foreign capital. Developers should monitor sponsor/founder backgrounds, especially in trophy markets (Georgetown, TX), where promotional overreach and misrepresentation are elevated risks.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development planning 740-acre Wildhaven luxury resort in Denton, TX; $350–$450M total value
152 homes priced $1.5M–$3M+; six miles horse trails, 100-key hotel, spa, 10-acre bass fishing pond
400 acres developed, 300+ preserved; Noble Classic Homes to build first phase; HKS design
stories.summary: Sarasota-based An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre master-planned luxury resort in Denton with $350–$450 million in total value. The project will include 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), a 100-key hotel, equestrian facilities, spa, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond, with 300+ acres preserved for natural space. Noble Classic Homes will build the first phase; Wildhaven-branded projects are also planned in Colorado and Florida.
stories.impact: Wildhaven reflects growing developer interest in lifestyle-centric, nature-themed luxury development in DFW suburban metros, driven by population inflow and affluent household growth in Denton. However, this is a for-sale luxury home and hospitality play, not multifamily rental development. The project signals strong demand for premium lifestyle experiences and experiential amenities (equestrian, fishing, trails) in Sunbelt high-growth markets, which multifamily developers should consider in master-planned community and luxury-mixed-use contexts. Monitor Denton employment and population growth for rental demand spillover into adjacent markets.

stories.title: Pure Paws Veterinary Care to Relocate to 5K SF at Brooklyn’s 1 Putnam Avenue
stories.excerpt: A pack of Brooklyn veterinarians are taking themselves for a short walk. Pure Paws Veterinary Care is relocating its Brooklyn practice to a larger, nearby location in the Clinton Hill neighborhood. The veterinary office signed a 5,000-square-foot lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, near the intersection of Cambridge Place and Fulton Street. The asking rent for the 10-year lease, signed July 1, was $75 per square foot. Traded first reported the deal. The mixed-use building’s landlord is Abe Betesh. Gabriel and Solomon Betesh of commercial brokerage Momentum Realty Group negotiated the deal on the landlord’s behalf. “We are excited to welcome Pure Paws Veterinary to a new and expanded site to service Clinton Hill,” the brokers said in a joint statement. “We believe in the continued growth of the neighborhood and are proud to play a role in strengthening and enhancing its retail landscape by bringing high-quality businesses to the community.” Pure Paws Veterinary Care currently operates three New York City locations, at 506 West 42nd Street in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen, at 350 Hudson Street in Hudson Square and at 944 Fulton Street in Brooklyn’s Clinton Hill, according to its website. It is unclear when the practice will relocate from its current Clinton Hill address, which is less than a block from its future location.. Babson Real Estate Advisors‘ Richard Bradbury represented the practice in the lease negotiations. Bradbury told Commercial Observer in a statement that Babson was pleased to represent the Pure Paws. “It will give them the opportunity to better serve their loyal and growing customer base for years to come,” Babson said. A representative for Pure Paws did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Pure Paws Veterinary Care relocates to 5K SF at 1 Putnam Avenue, Brooklyn; $75/SF asking rent
10-year lease signed July 1 in Clinton Hill neighborhood; deal negotiated by Momentum Realty Group brokers
Expansion signals neighborhood retail/healthcare services growth and landlord confidence in Clinton Hill demand
stories.summary: Pure Paws Veterinary Care signed a 10-year lease for 5,000 square feet at 1 Putnam Avenue in Brooklyn's Clinton Hill neighborhood at $75 per square foot. The relocation from nearby represents a neighborhood expansion and signals increased veterinary/healthcare tenant demand in the submarket. Momentum Realty Group brokers negotiated the deal on behalf of landlord Abe Betesh.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a small-format retail/services lease in a secondary Brooklyn neighborhood. The $75/SF rent reflects tight retail availability in Clinton Hill but signals no broader residential demand or supply trends. However, the expansion of neighborhood services (veterinary, retail) into previously underserved Clinton Hill blocks may indicate gentrification/demographic upgrading, which could correlate with upward rent pressure in the submarket; monitor for broader neighborhood health-and-wellness/luxury retail clustering as an early-stage demographic signal.

stories.title: Bridges Development Group Buys Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M
stories.excerpt: Kingswood Center in Brooklyn’s Midwood neighborhood has a new owner, Commercial Observer has learned. The 229,926-square-foot shopping center at 1630 East 15th Street sold for $31 million to Michael Berfield’s Bridges Development Group, according to broker JLL. The sale marks a steep discount from the $89 million that the shopping center fetched in early 2020, the last time it traded. The three-story, Class B property is currently anchored by T.J. Maxx. A LoopNet listing advertises nearly 100,000 square feet of retail and commercial space available for lease. Kingswood Center was constructed in 2007 and renovated in 2019, according to the listing, and features an on-site parking garage. JLL’s Ethan Stanton, Michael Mazzara and Brendan Maddigan represented the seller, PNC’s Midland Loan Services, in direct negotiations with the buyer. A JLL debt team of Scott Aiese and Alex Staikos secured $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer. The firm declined to disclose the lender. The financing will allow Bridges Development Group to execute a business plan of investing in the property and leasing its vacant space, according to a JLL spokesperson. Midland took possession of the property from its former owner, retail shopping REIT Urban Edge Properties, in 2023. The REIT fell behind on a $66 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan used to acquire the center in February 2020. Following the ill-timed purchase, the shopping center’s annual net operating income fell to $1.9 million and occupancy declined to 35 percent, The Real Deal reported at the time. Urban Edge Properties still owns the nearby Kingswood Crossing at 1715 East 13th Street, according to its website. Spokespeople for Bridges Development Group and PNC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bridges Development Group acquires Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M; 65% discount from 2020 price of $89M
229.9K SF Class B shopping center in Midwood anchored by T.J. Maxx; 100K SF vacant/available
JLL secured $34.65M acquisition financing; seller PNC's Midland Loan Services in distressed liquidation
stories.summary: Bridges Development Group purchased Kingswood Center, a 229,926-square-foot shopping center in Brooklyn's Midwood, for $31 million—a 65% discount from its $89 million sale price in early 2020. The Class B property, anchored by T.J. Maxx, currently has nearly 100,000 square feet of vacant retail/commercial space. JLL arranged $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer.
stories.impact: Kingswood Center's steep valuation decline (from $89M to $31M) reflects national retail distress and asset-liability management by distressed lender PNC. However, for multifamily developers, this signals potential opportunity: class B retail centers in secondary Brooklyn neighborhoods (Midwood) may be targets for ground-floor repositioning in mixed-use conversions or intensive redevelopment. The low acquisition cost and large vacant footprint suggest Bridges is positioning for a value-add or conversion play; monitor the property for mixed-use/residential redevelopment signals, which could accelerate if the submarket gentrifies or transit access improves.

stories.title: Data Intelligence Firm Curinos Takes 14K SF at Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central
stories.excerpt: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial industry, is relocating to Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central after signing a 14,000-square-foot lease, Commercial Observer has learned. Curinos will move from its current office about two blocks north at 485 Lexington Avenue, though it is unclear when the move will happen. The length of the lease was not disclosed. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central — which is now 98 percent leased, according to the landlord — ranges from $85 to $135 per square foot. Adam Ardise and Rachel Rosenfeld from Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos in the lease, while JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Thomas Swartz, Carlee Palmer and Margaux Kelleher represented Marx Realty. Spokespeople for C&W and JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. “The robust leasing activity at 10 Grand Central demonstrates the ongoing appeal of thoughtfully designed workplaces and white glove service that combine to give companies an elevated office experience,” Craig Deitelzweig, CEO of Marx Realty, said in a statement. “Curinos’ decision to relocate within the Grand Central market speaks to the differentiated hospitality-infused experience we’ve created at 10 Grand Central and further strengthens the building’s exceptional tenant roster,” Deitelzweig added. 10 Grand Central is a hospitality-infused office building that offers tenants a variety of amenities, including a reservable house car for transportation, a lounge and cafe on the seventh floor, a 1930s French garden-style outdoor terrace, and a newly opened speakeasy-style restaurant called Highball Ltd. Other tenants at 10 Grand Central include data center developer Edged, bank holding company Merchants Bancorp and research firm Green Street Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Curinos, data intelligence firm, relocates to 14K SF lease at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central, Manhattan
Building now 98% leased; asking rents range $85–$135/SF; move from 485 Lexington Avenue (two blocks north)
Cushman & Wakefield and JLL brokered lease; CEO highlights 'elevated office experience' and white-glove service
stories.summary: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm, signed a 14,000-square-foot lease at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central in Manhattan after relocating from nearby 485 Lexington Avenue. The building is now 98% leased with asking rents of $85–$135 per square foot. The transaction reflects strong demand for premium Midtown office space and Marx Realty's successful repositioning of the asset.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a premium office lease in Manhattan and signals class A Midtown office strength and landlord investment in amenities/experience. High occupancy (98%) and robust rents ($85–$135/SF) reflect a bifurcated Manhattan office market where trophy assets attract capital while secondary stock languishes. No direct impact on residential supply, demand, or capital. However, strong financial services employment (Curinos serves finance clients) in Midtown suggests sustained workforce demand, which supports underlying Manhattan residential demand for market-rate apartments.
